title: Code LLMs: A Taxonomy-based Survey
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across various NLP tasks and have recently expanded their impact to coding tasks, bridging the gap between natural languages (NL) and programming languages (PL). This taxonomy-based survey provides a comprehensive analysis of LLMs in the NL-PL domain, investigating how these models are utilized in coding tasks and examining their methodologies, architectures, and training processes. We propose a taxonomy-based framework that categorizes relevant concepts, providing a unified classification system to facilitate a deeper understanding of this rapidly evolving field. This survey offers insights into the current state and future directions of LLMs in coding tasks, including their applications and limitations.
extracted_text:
Code LLMs: A Taxonomy-based Survey
Nishat Raihan
George Mason University
Fairfax, VA, USA
mraihan2@gmu.edu
Christian Newman
Rochester Institute of Technology
Rochester, NY, USA
cdnvse@rit.edu
Marcos Zampieri
George Mason University
Fairfax, VA, USA
mzampier@gmu.edu
Abstract—Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated
remarkable capabilities across various NLP tasks and have
recently expanded their impact to coding tasks, bridging the gap
between natural languages (NL) and programming languages (PL).
This taxonomy-based survey provides a comprehensive analysis
of LLMs in the NL-PL domain, investigating how these models
are utilized in coding tasks and examining their methodologies,
architectures, and training processes. We propose a taxonomy-
based framework that categorizes relevant concepts, providing a
unified classification system to facilitate a deeper understanding
of this rapidly evolving field. This survey offers insights into
the current state and future directions of LLMs in coding tasks,
including their applications and limitations.
Index Terms—Large Language Models (LLMs), Code-LLMS,
Code Generation, Corpora, Benchmark
I. INTRODUCTION
LLMs pretrained on very large corpora [1], show adaptability
to various NLP tasks, including challenging NL-PL tasks.
Models built on top of them, such as CodeBERT [2] and
GraphCodeBERT [3] have been instrumental in bridging human
and computational language processing.
Causally masked, decoder-only language models such as
GPT [1] are introduced before encoder-only models like BERT
[4] or Electra [5]. However, they garner significant attention
with the release of OpenAI’s GPT-2 [6] and subsequent GPT-3
[7] models, demonstrating efficiency in a wide range of NLP
tasks, including zero-shot learning and programming language
comprehension. OpenAI also introduces HumanEval [7] - a
benchmark for code generation. Despite their capabilities, these
models are proprietary, restricting further experimentation by
the research community. In response, open-source alternatives
like Falcon [8] and MetaAI’s LLaMA [9] are developed.
They leverage sophisticated training techniques such as Rotary
Positional Embeddings [10], Grouped Multi Query Attention,
and the SwiGLU activation function [11], aiming to offset
their parameter deficit. However, they typically underperform
compared to their closed-source counterparts like GPT-4 and
Anthropic’s Claude [12] on coding benchmarks - most likely
due to the use of public corpora like Wikipedia [13] and
Common Crawl [14], which lack a rich representation of
programming languages.
The contributions of this paper are as follows.
• We present the first taxonomy-based survey on Code
LLMs.
• We examine decoder-based models as well as encoder and
encoder-decoder architectures, providing in-depth analysis
of model structures and nuances.
• We discuss relevant benchmarks and corpora, addressing
potential challenges and their solutions.
• We provide insights on open problems in the field in order
to move forward.
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Section
II presents a comprehensive taxonomy of the field, encompass-
ing tasks, datasets, models, benchmarks, and challenges, to
provide a holistic view. Section III offers a critical analysis of
the literature, comparing and contrasting different approaches.
Section IV focuses on unresolved issues and open problems
within the domain, pointing to directions for future research.
Throughout these sections, we will consistently refer back to
our primary objectives, ensuring that our analysis provides
clear insights into the educational challenges [15], ethical
considerations, and performance trends of LLMs in coding
tasks.
II. AREA TAXONOMY
Figure 1 presents a comprehensive taxonomy of research in
the area of Code Large Language Models (Code LLMs). The
taxonomy organizes the field into five principal sub-areas:
1) Tasks: We classify coding tasks into four distinct
categories based on the nature of their inputs and
outputs, which may involve Natural Language (NL)
and/or Programming Language (PL). These categories
are:
• NL-NL: Tasks where both input and output are in
natural language.
• NL-PL: Tasks where input is in natural language,
and output is in a programming language.
• PL-PL: Tasks where both input and output are in a
programming language.
• PL-NL: Tasks where input is in a programming
language, and output is in natural language.
This classification provides a systematic framework to
organize and focus on various coding tasks effectively.
Our survey emphasizes Code LLMs’ performance in
generative tasks (NL-PL), particularly code generation
from human language prompts.
2) Corpora: The success of transformer-based models
hinges on pretraining with large, diverse datasets. For
Code LLMs, corpora are categorized into:
arXiv:2412.08291v1  [cs.CL]  11 Dec 2024


Fig. 1: Code LLMs - Area Taxonomy.
• PL-corpora: Datasets composed of programming
languages.
• NL-corpora: Datasets consisting of natural language
text.
Recent efforts have focused on compiling specialized pro-
gramming corpora to enhance the models’ understanding
of coding tasks.
3) Models: We analyze three primary types of Transformer-
based models [16]:
• Encoder-only models: Typically used for under-
standing tasks.
• Decoder-only models: Primarily effective for gener-
ative tasks, which are the focus of this survey.
• Encoder-decoder models: Employed for tasks re-
quiring both understanding and generation.
Decoder-only models are further categorized into:
• Foundational models: Pretrained on extensive cor-
pora and categorized based on their data sources as
base, code, or mixed.
• Fine-tuned models: Adapted for specific tasks to
improve performance.
4) Benchmarks: The proliferation of Code LLMs necessi-
tates robust benchmarks to evaluate their performance.
These benchmarks serve as critical tools for comparing
models and assessing their capabilities across various
tasks.
5) Challenges: Finally, we highlight the key challenges in
this domain, including:
• Potential limitations in existing models and corpora.
• Ethical concerns surrounding the use of generated
code.
• Real-world applicability and the need for more
interpretable, reliable models.
We also propose directions for addressing these chal-
lenges to ensure sustainable advancements in the field.
This taxonomy provides a structured framework for navi-
gating the research landscape, highlighting interconnections
between sub-areas, and fostering a systematic approach to
future research and development in Code LLMs.
III. TAXONOMY-BASED SURVEY
Now we take a deeper dive into each of these subdomains
and analyze them further.
A. Tasks
The spectrum of relevant tasks in the domain can be
categorized into four main types: NL-NL, PL-NL, PL-PL,
and NL-PL. While each category serves distinct purposes, our
primary focus lies on NL-PL tasks, particularly code generation,


which showcases the primary purpose of decoder-based Code
LLMs.
Earlier works include NL-NL tasks mainly, such as doc-
ument translation and log analysis, traditionally driven by
standard NLP techniques. As code-specific applications gained
prominence, PL-NL tasks like code summarization, comment
generation, and identifier normalization emerged [17]–[19],
bridging the gap between code and human understanding.
Early approaches used statistical methods, but neural networks
now offer greater accuracy. Identifier normalization, including
abbreviation expansion and variable name splitting [20]–[22],
is essential for improving code comprehension and quality.
Latter works explore PL-PL tasks focus on refining existing
code, including translation, completion, infilling, refactoring
[23], and code smell detection [24]. Models such as CodeBERT
[2] and GraphCodeBERT [3] have excelled in tasks like code
infilling by framing it as a masked language modeling problem.
Current works focus on advanced NL-PL tasks, notably
code generation, where models must interpret natural language
instructions and programming concepts, including syntax,
semantics, and frameworks, to produce executable code. These
models are evaluated by how accurately generated code
passes test cases. Recent advances in autoregressive models,
particularly GPT-4 [25], have improved performance across
all task types, though challenges remain in complex areas
like code translation and ensuring the correctness of generated
code. As research progresses, Code LLMs continue to enhance
automation and augment software development processes.

B. Corpora
General purpose pretrained models get trained on large cor-
pora of text which often lack a rich distribution of programming
languages. Also, these corpora filter out code-related data,
which enables superior natural language understanding but
often results in suboptimal performance on coding tasks. To
improve competency in the programming domain, some code-
focused corpora have been compiled in recent years as well.
These are compiled using publicly available code repositories
like GitHub1 or Kaggle2. Most recently, some of the LLMs
also use synthetic corpora generated by other LLMs, as shown
in Table I. The Code LLMs incorporate training on these code-
heavy resources along with general-purpose corpora. As shown
1www.github.com
2www.kaggle.com
in Table I, we categorize the most used training corpora into
two subdomains: NL and PL.
C. Models
Building on transformer architectures [16], several works
have explored encoder-only (CodeBERT [2]), encoder-decoder
(CodeT5 [26]), and decoder-only variants. Currently, decoder-
only models like GPT4 [25] and CodeLLaMA [27] achieve
state-of-the-art results in code generation tasks. We provide an
in-depth analysis for each of these model types.
1) Encoders: The foundational encoder-only model, BERT
[4], uses masked token prediction and next sentence prediction
as the pretraining tasks. They evolved to be just masked
span detection (ERNIE [28]) and corrupted token prediction
(ELECTRA [5]) for efficiency gains. As shown in Table II,
iterative innovations in masking objectives have cumulatively
enhanced representation learning and downstream performance
in coding tasks.

TABLE II: Masked Language Models [Encoders] and the type
of Masked Tokens during their pre-training phase.
Earlier encoders such as CuBERT [30] use unified pretraining
(NL-NL or PL-PL pairs) and MLM as the pretraining task.
While CodeBERT [2] focuses on both MLM and corrupted
token prediction. During the pretraining phase, it uses both
unimodal (NL-NL or PL-PL pairs) and bimodal (NL-PL pairs)
to have a better understanding of the coding contexts. Latter
models include TreeBERT [31] that modifies the MLM task as
a Tree MLM (TMLM) task, where instead of predicting missing
NL or PL tokens, the model tries to predict the missing nodes
of the Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs). ASTs are generated
using open source tools like tree-sitter3. Combining these
methods, GraphCodeBERT [3] includes the ASTs along with
the NL-PL sentence pair to provide more structural context
for the code. This trio-MLM structure is further improved by
SynCoBERT [32], with two newly introduced pre-training tasks
called Identifier Prediction, where the models attempt to predict
which tokens from the source code are identifiers (Identifiers in
source code refer to the names given to programmatic elements
like variables, functions, etc. which are used to represent and
reference them within code.) and AST Edge Prediction, where
the model predicts the edges in the ASTs provided. Further
improved by Code-MVP [33] with the inclusion of Control

Flow Graphs (CFGS) along with NL-PL-AST trio. CFGs are
constructed using publicly available tools like Scalpel [40]. In
Table II, we list the types of masking these models employ.
Encoder-only models excel in tasks like documentation gen-
eration, code search, summarization, and defect detection, but
comparing their performance is challenging due to inconsistent
reporting across tasks, evaluation metrics, and datasets. In Table
III, we focus on benchmarks with the broadest reporting, using
a uniform accuracy metric.
2) Encoder-Decoders: These models employ a full trans-
former architecture like T5 [41] and BART [42]. Building
on T5, models like PyMT5 [43] and CodeT5 [26] have been
proposed for coding tasks in Python. CodeT5 incorporates
identifier prediction and finetuning on tasks like code generation
and summarization. Latter works like CodeT5+ [44] and SPT-
Code [45] perform two-stage pretraining, first on unimodal
then bimodal data, adding ASTs for better understanding.
Deepmind’s AlphaCode [46] achieves state-of-the-art code
generation via larger models, scaling up to 41B parameters,
pretrained on high-quality GitHub data. Other works build
on top of BART [42], such as PLBART [47] which unifies
unimodal and bimodal pretraining. Unlike encoders, these
models show good code-generation capability, as we mention
some of them in Table IV, compared with decoders.
3) Decoders: The decoder-only models are the current SOTA
in code generation, as shown in Table IV. They have several
variants with different architectural nuances. We can broadly
categorize them into two - foundational and finetuned.
a) Foundational: Most notable mentions include the GPT
series [1] [6] [7] [25], which evolved from minimal finetuning
of GPT1 to zero-shot SOTA results of GPT4. The LLaMA
series [9] [69] also represents major strides through optimized
performance and efficiency.
Based on the pretraining procedure, we categorize these
foundational models into three:
1) Base: These models are pretrained on extensive corpora
and designed for general tasks. Large base models like
GPT-4 [25], Claude 3 [51], Gemini [61], LLaMA (70B)
[69], and Falcon (180B) [8] excel at code generation,
likely due to their substantial parameters and pretraining
data, as supported by the Chinchilla Scaling Hypothesis
[70]. This hypothesis posits that performance improves
in proportion to the number of parameters and training
tokens. In practice, smaller models like LLaMA (7B) [69],
GPT-2 [6], and Falcon (7B) [8] underperform compared
to larger ones. However, innovative architectures allow
smaller models to remain competitive. The LLaMA
authors [9] found their 7B model continued to improve
even after training on 1 trillion tokens, challenging
conventional scaling laws.
Unlike GPT models [6] [48], which use Layer Normal-
ization, LLaMA [9] [69] employs RMS Normalization
for better efficiency. LayerNorm normalizes input across
features by adjusting it based on mean and variance, but
it can be computationally slow. RMSNorm, on the other
hand, normalizes using the root mean square of input
values, reducing training time while maintaining perfor-
mance. Most open-source LLMs use RMSNorm, with
Falcon being an exception [8], as it retains LayerNorm.
LLaMA models [69] adopt Rotary Positional Embeddings
(RoPE) [71] instead of absolute or relative embeddings.
Absolute Positional Embeddings [16], used in early
transformers, encode token positions with sine and cosine
functions, while Relative Positional Embeddings [72],
used in models like GPT [25] and Falcon [8], consider
distances between tokens. RoPE combines these, offering
improved positional information handling.
Notably, smaller models like Mistral (7B) outperform
larger models like LLaMA (34B) [69] in code generation,
likely due to the Sliding Window Attention mechanism,
which enhances long-sequence processing without the
quadratic complexity of traditional Causal Masking
Attention. This mechanism limits the attention scope
to a local window, increasing efficiency, as seen in larger
Mistral models [73].
Recent advances in open-source base models include
Mistral Large 1 and 2 [53], [54], DBRX [58], Command
R+ [74], and Nemotron [57]. Mistral Large 2 excels in
code generation, supporting over 80 programming and
13 natural languages, outperforming larger models like
LLaMA 3.1 (405B) on specific benchmarks. It’s fine-
tuned to reduce hallucinations and improve reasoning,
making it versatile for developers and researchers.
DBRX [58] focuses on large-scale code generation and
debugging, while Command R+ [74] enhances real-time
coding productivity through suggestions and optimiza-

tions. Nemotron [57] specializes in code generation for
niche domains like scientific computing and financial
modeling, with advanced function-calling capabilities for
precise task execution.
2) Code: General-purpose models like Falcon [8] and
LLaMA [9] tend to underperform in code generation
due to limited source code in their pretraining corpora.
In contrast, models like Code-LLaMA [9], SantaCoder
[68], and StarCoder [66] leverage corpora with a higher
proportion of source code, such as CodeParrot (1TB)
[75], which contains about 80
The phi-series utilizes Multi-Head Attention (MHA),
which processes information from different subspaces
simultaneously. Meanwhile, SantaCoder and StarCoder
adopt Multi-Query Attention (MQA), where multiple
queries are handled at once, a method also used in GPT-
3 [48]. Though more efficient, MQA trades off some
performance compared to MHA.
3) Base + Code: Larger base models excel in code
generation but benefit from additional pretraining on code
corpora. LLaMA2 evolves into Code-LLaMA [27] after
further training on 859 GB of code, while DeepMind’s
PaLM-Coder [65] adopts a similar approach with a 540B
parameter model.
While PaLM-Coder employs MQA, Code-LLaMA intro-
duces Grouped Multi-Query Attention, which processes
queries in groups, reducing computational complexity
while maintaining efficiency. LLaMA models also opti-
mize resource usage through KV-caching, storing keys
and values to reduce computational demand for long
sequences, accelerating inference during text generation
[67].
Both
PaLM-Coder
and
Code-LLaMA
implement
SwiGLU activations [76], a blend of Swish [77] and GLU
[78], which has proven more effective than traditional
ReLU [79] activations.
b) Finetuned: Due to the high costs and resources
required for pretraining LLMs, researchers often enhance base
or code-pretrained models with supervised finetuning for code
generation. Even finetuning smaller LLMs (<7B parameters)
demands significant resources, prompting the development of
efficient finetuning methods.
One such method is Precision & Quantization, which re-
duces model precision from formats like FP32 to FP16 or
INT8, decreasing memory usage and speeding up computa-
tion. Quantization further compresses models by discretizing
continuous values. However, while improving efficiency, these
techniques may reduce model accuracy, necessitating careful
trade-offs in resource-constrained environments.
Another approach is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) [80],
which modifies the model’s weights by adding low-rank updates
without significantly changing the pretrained weights. This
method allows efficient adaptation while retaining the learned
structure of the base model.
QLoRA [81] extends LoRA by quantizing the low-rank ma-
trices, further reducing the memory footprint and computational
resources required for both training and inference, making it
especially suitable for deployment in resource-limited settings.
Several works have focused on LLMs finetuned on foun-
dational models for coding tasks. CodeLLama (7B) and


CodeLLaMA-Python [27] by MetaAI employ a unique ap-
proach, using larger models to generate synthetic code corpora
for finetuning smaller 7B models. WizardCoder [56] builds on
the EvolInstruct method from WizardLM [82], adapting it for
coding tasks and finetuning StarCoder [66] with challenging
prompts, resulting in models that outperform most open-
source code-LLMs on HumanEval, aligning with the Scaling
Hypothesis [70]. OctoCoder [83] also adopts StarCoder [66]
as its base, finetuning it on the CommitPack [70] dataset
using LoRA [80], reporting improved results compared to
WizardCoder.
Phind-CodeLLaMA [60] is fine-tuned on a proprietary
dataset of high-quality programming problems and solutions,
eschewing LoRA [80] in its fine-tuning process. MagiCoder
[59], the smallest open-source model to surpass Code-LLaMA
(70B), constructs the OSSInstruct [59] dataset using GPT3.5
[48] to generate higher-quality problem descriptions, then
finetunes CodeLLaMA (7B) [27] without LoRA [80], achieving
a higher HumanEval [48] score than GPT3.5.
CodeStral [53], a 22B model by Mistral AI, supports
over 80 programming languages and excels in various code
generation tasks, outperforming other models in benchmarks
like HumanEval and RepoBench. Lastly, CodeGemma [84],
derived from Gemma [85], offers 2B and 7B variants with
base and instruction-tuned versions, trained on permissively-
licensed code and coding-related web pages. It demonstrates
strong performance across code-related tasks and employs
responsible AI practices.
Most finetuned models tend to focus on the most widely
used PLs (Python, JAVA etc.), with limited focus on less used
and newly introduced ones. Mojo-Coder [86] being the only
exception that focuses on the newly introduced Mojo language,
mainly used in the AI domain.
D. Benchmarks
Encoder-only models address diverse coding tasks, while
decoder-only and encoder-decoder models focus on generative
capabilities. Comparing encoder-only models is challenging
due to varied tasks and selective reporting. Code Generation,
however, benefits from established benchmarks like HumanEval
[48] and MBPP [50], using the standardized pass@k metric
[87]. This metric measures the probability that at least one
of k-generated code samples passes all test cases, enabling
uniform evaluation of models’ task-solving abilities.

TABLE V: Overview of Code Generation Benchmarks
The most widely used ones are shown in Table V; these
provide a comprehensive framework for evaluating code
generation capabilities across various programming languages
and problem types. HumanEval [48] serves as the primary
benchmark, with HumanEval+ [49], HumanEval-XL [88] and
mHumanEval [89] offering expanded versions. MBPP [50] and
its extension MBPP+ [49] focus on Python programming prob-
lems. Multipl-E [90] extends support to multiple programming
languages, DS-1000 [91] offers diverse, real-world prompts
from StackOverflow and CSEPrompts [92], [93] focus on
prompts from academic settings.
E. Challenges
Code LLMs face numerous challenges in development and
deployment [94], [95].
1) Pitfalls: Data quality issues, including sampling bias,
noise, and labeling errors, affect model generalization [68], [96].
Design limitations such as data leakage, spurious correlations,
and inappropriate architectures hinder performance [68], [97].
Evaluation is complicated by inappropriate baselines, unrealistic
benchmarks, and improper metrics [49], [90]. Deployment
challenges include resource constraints, latency requirements,
and security threats [94], [98]. Additionally, models may pro-
duce buggy code that is syntactically correct but semantically
incorrect or insecure [99].
2) Implications: These pitfalls lead to overestimated model
performance, difficulty in reproducing results, and vulnerability
to attacks such as evasion, data poisoning, and extraction [100].
3) Potential Solutions: Improving data quality through
initiatives like The Stack [101] and phi-series [62] is crucial.
Preventing data leakage and standardizing evaluation methods
can enhance result reliability [95]. Incorporating code structures
such as syntax trees and control flow graphs may improve
model performance [3], [32]. Developing robust models and
integrating code analysis tools can address security concerns
[98].
Our paper contributes by highlighting the need for realistic
benchmarks, consistent evaluation metrics, and improved data
quality and model robustness.
IV. OPEN PROBLEMS
While the field of Code LLMs is rapidly evolving, frequently
overcoming complex challenges and achieving new state-of-
the-art results, several open problems require attention. In this
section, we draw conclusions from our survey to highlight these
issues, thereby addressing the goals outlined in the introduction.
A. Regarding the Benchmarks
a) Current benchmarks do not capture real-world
scenarios: Benchmarks like HumanEval [48] and MBPP [50]
do not fully capture real-world coding scenarios. There is a
need for benchmarks that represent complex multi-function
and multi-file problems, tasks with vague specifications, and
require documentation skills to better evaluate the models.


b) LLMs need more coverage for a larger range of
programming languages: While most benchmarks are intended
for Python, some efforts to broaden them to include more
programming languages have been made. Multiple-E [90]
and HumanEval-XL [88] contain prompts for 18 and 12
programming languages respectively, still missing out on many
widely used ones4 like Visual Basic, MATLAB, Fortran, etc.
These multilingual benchmarks also show a disproportionate
focus skewed more towards Python compared to the rest. There
are approximately 9,000 programming languages globally56,
more than the number of natural languages (6,909).7 With
at least 250 actively used daily and 100 having a large user
base8, there is a clear need for benchmarks that cover more
programming languages.
c) Benchmarks may cause LLMs to become biased
toward the way they are tested: While public benchmarks
benefit research, they also risk data memorization by allowing
Code LLMs to fine-tune on them and achieve misleadingly
high results. An ongoing concern in the domain is whether
the LLMs are already fine-tuned on the public benchmarks.
Introducing benchmarks with blind test sets could help prevent
data leakage and model bias toward test data.
d) LLMs are currently limited in their support for the
vast majority of human languages: LLMs are primarily trained
on English data, with notable exceptions like GPT-4 [25],
BLOOM [102], and the recent Aya model [103]. However, most
LLMs underperform in non-English languages, as indicated
by Yiqiao et al. [104]. Further study is needed to determine if
this shortcoming extends to coding tasks as well. It is crucial
to know if a Code LLM is as effective when the coding task
is described in French or any non-English language.
B. Regarding the Models
a) Effectiveness of synthetic data suggests quality over
quantity: Recent studies, such as the phi-series [62], [63]
and Magicoder [59], have demonstrated the effectiveness of
using synthetic datasets for pre-training and fine-tuning. These
approaches have yielded impressive results, suggesting that the
quality of programming language data may be more crucial
than its quantity. Consequently, these findings raise important
questions about the future of programming language dataset
curation, prompting researchers to reconsider the necessity
of large, noisy corpora and explore the potential benefits of
focusing on carefully crafted synthetic ones.
b) Need for resource-efficient Code LLMs without
performance sacrifice: LLMs, even smaller (<7B parameters)
ones, are computationally very demanding. Adaptation methods
like LoRA [80] and QLoRA [81] help but may compromise
performance, requiring further research to minimize this
sacrifice. Moreover, the recent success of 1-bit LLMs with
4https://www.tiobe.com/tiobe-index/
5https://devskiller.com/blog/how-many-programming-languages/
6https://www.epitech-it.es/how-many-programming-languages-are/
7https://www.linguisticsociety.org/content/how-many-languages-are-there-world
8https://www.tiobe.com/tiobe-index/
BitNet [105] invites exploration in the context of Code LLMs,
potentially significantly reducing resource requirements.
c) Trade-offs in multilingual Code LLMs: Do they lose
PL-specific performance?: The mBERT [4] model, pretrained
on 104 natural languages, shows a performance drop for most
languages compared to the monolingual BERT models, which
is later mitigated by XLM-RoBERTa [106]. As Code LLMs are
pretrained and/or fine-tuned on more and more programming
languages, it is important to investigate whether they lose any
language-specific performance. If so, research is needed on
how to reduce this loss.
d) Re-evaluating the Scaling Hypothesis for Code
LLMs: The Chinchilla scaling hypothesis [70] posits a linear
relationship between tokens and parameters, primarily observed
in general LLMs. Given the phi-1 model’s [62] success with
higher-quality data over sheer size, there is a need to reconsider
the scaling hypothesis for Code LLMs with a focus on data
quality. Further study is warranted on what constitutes quality
data in this context.
e) Do Code LLMs perform worse on general tasks?:
Code LLMs are often pretrained on corpora that have sig-
nificantly more programming language content compared to
natural language data. Does this make them perform worse on
more general tasks aside from coding tasks? Additionally, do
code fine-tuned models become worse than their base versions
at regular tasks? These questions require further investigation.
V. CONCLUSION
This paper presents a comprehensive survey of Code Lan-
guage Models (Code LLMs), organized around a carefully
developed taxonomy that encapsulates the core aspects of the
field. By tracing the evolution of Code LLMs, the survey
distills the most significant concepts into a clear and structured
framework, which is discussed in detail. Each subdomain
within Code LLMs is examined thoroughly, identifying current
research challenges, gaps in knowledge, and offering insights
into potential solutions that could drive future advancements.
Beyond the conceptual analysis, the survey provides an exten-
sive review of existing models, focusing on their architectural
intricacies, training methodologies, and fine-tuning techniques.
The survey is current, incorporating the most recent models,
datasets, and research findings as of August 2024, ensuring its
relevance to ongoing developments in the field.
The focus is on works that have been rigorously evaluated,
either through publication in well-regarded journals or val-
idation on recognized benchmarks and leaderboards. These
benchmarks and leaderboards are maintained by established
and influential communities, such as EvalPlus [107], AllenAI’s
WildBench9, 10 and HuggingFace’s BigCode LLM11. This
approach ensures that the survey captures the most credible
and impactful developments in the field.
Overall, this survey aims to serve as a valuable resource
for researchers and practitioners in the domain of Code LLMs.
9https://huggingface.co/spaces/allenai/WildBench
10https://github.com/LudwigStumpp/llm-leaderboard
11https://huggingface.co/spaces/bigcode/bigcode-models-leaderboard


By providing a clear understanding of the current state of the
art, highlighting open problems, and suggesting directions for
future research, it contributes to the advancement of knowledge
and practice in this rapidly evolving area.
